request.question: What was the population at the 2010 census of the city where the transmitter site for KQSP (1530 AM) is located?

title: Odessa, Texas
Sudislavsky District
Wentzville, Missouri
Roanoke, Indiana
Chanhassen, Minnesota
Kursk
Athens, Georgia
Brainerd, Minnesota
KQSP
North Laurel, Maryland
sentences: Odessa is a city in and the county seat of Ector County, Texas, United States.
It is located primarily in Ector County, although a small portion of the city extends into Midland County.
Odessa's population was 118,918 at the 2010 census making it the 29th-most populous city in Texas; estimates as of July 2015 indicate a population of 159,436 in the city.
It is the principal city of the Odessa Metropolitan Statistical Area, which includes all of Ector County.

The metropolitan area is also a component of the larger Midland–Odessa combined statistical area, which had a 2010 census population of 278,801; a recent report from the United States Census Bureau estimates that the combined population as of July 2015 is 320,513.
In 2014, "Forbes" magazine ranked Odessa as the third fastest-growing small city in the United States.
Sudislavsky District (Russian: Судисла́вский район ) is an administrative and municipal district (raion), one of the twenty-four in Kostroma Oblast, Russia.
It is located in the southwest of the oblast.
The area of the district is 1530 km2 .
Its administrative center is the urban locality (an urban-type settlement) of Sudislavl.
Population:  (2010 Census) ; 15,184 (2002 Census);  (1989 Census) The population of Sudislavl accounts for 37.6% of the district's total population.
Wentzville is a city located in western St. Charles County, Missouri, United States.
As of the 2010 census, the city had a total population of 29,070.
2016 population estimates have placed the city's population at 37,395, making it the 17th largest city in Missouri.

Wentzville was the fastest growing city in Missouri between 2000 and 2010, and population estimates since 2010 indicate that Wentzville is likely to continue as the state's fastest growing city for a second consecutive decade.
As the site of the county fairgrounds, Wentzville hosts the annual St. Gerard County Parade (also known as the "Black Parade"), St. Lazlo Wentz Festival and the Greater St. Louis Renaissance Faire.
Roanoke is a town in Jackson Township, Huntington County, Indiana, United States.
The population was 1,722 at the 2010 census.
Roanoke is governed by a town council.
Town offices include the clerk treasurer, utilities department, police department, and volunteer fire department.
The town has a public elementary school.
WOWO and WBNI have transmitter towers in Roanoke, along U.S. Highway 24.
For many years, television station WPTA was licensed to Roanoke, though that station's studios and transmitter have always been located in nearby Fort Wayne.
Chanhassen is a city in Carver and Hennepin counties in the state of Minnesota.
It is southwest of Minneapolis.
The population was 22,952 at the 2010 census.
Kursk (Russian: Курск ; ] ) is a city and the administrative center of Kursk Oblast, Russia, located at the confluence of the Kur, Tuskar, and Seym Rivers.
The area around Kursk was the site of a turning point in the Soviet–German struggle during World War II and the site of the largest tank battle in history.
Population:  (2010 Census) ;  (2002 Census) ;  (1989 Census)

Athens (formally known as Athens-Clarke County) is a consolidated city–county in the U.S. state of Georgia, in the northeastern part of the state about an hour's drive from the global city of Atlanta, and comprising the former city of Athens proper (the county seat) and Clarke County.
The University of Georgia, the state's flagship public research university, is located in this college town, and contributed to its initial growth.
In 1991, after a vote the preceding year, the original city abandoned its charter to form a unified government with Clarke County, referred to jointly as Athens-Clarke County.

As of the 2010 census, the consolidated city-county (including all of Athens-Clarke County except Winterville and a portion of Bogart) had a total population of 115,452; all of Clarke County had a population of 116,714.
Athens is the sixth-largest city in Georgia, and the principal city of the Athens-Clarke County, Georgia Metropolitan Statistical Area, which had a population of 192,541 as of the 2010 census.
Athens-Clarke County has the smallest geographical area of a county in Georgia.
Brainerd is a city in Crow Wing County, Minnesota, United States.
Its population was 13,592 as of the 2010 census.
It is the county seat of Crow Wing County, and is one of the largest cities in Central Minnesota.
Brainerd straddles the Mississippi River several miles upstream from its confluence with the Crow Wing River, having been founded as a site for a railroad crossing above said confluence.
Brainerd is the principal city of the Brainerd Micropolitan Area, a micropolitan area covering Cass and Crow Wing counties and with a combined population of 91,067 as of the 2010 census.
The Brainerd area serves as a major tourist destination for Minnesota, and Baxter is a regional retail center.
KQSP (1530 AM) is a radio station licensed to Shakopee, Minnesota and serving the western suburbs of Minneapolis.
It is owned by Broadcast One, Inc., and airs a tropical format.
The station's studios are located in Golden Valley and the transmitter site is in Chanhassen.
North Laurel is a census-designated place (CDP) in Howard County, Maryland, United States.
The published population was 4,474 at the 2010 census.
This population was substantially less than the CDP's population in 2000, and was the result of an error in defining the boundary prior to tabulation and publication of 2010 Census results.
The corrected 2010 Census population is 20,259.
North Laurel is located adjacent to the city of Laurel.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What was the population at the 2010 census of the city where the transmitter site for KQSP (1530 AM) is located?
hop1_docs: KQSP | KQSP (1530 AM) is a radio station licensed to Shakopee, Minnesota and serving the western suburbs of Minneapolis.  It is owned by Broadcast One, Inc., and airs a tropical format.  The station's studios are located in Golden Valley and the transmitter site is in Chanhassen.

WNYC Transmitter Park | WNYC Transmitter Park is a 6.61-acre public park located where Greenpoint Avenue dead-ends by the East River shoreline, in the Greenpoint neighborhood of Brooklyn, New York.  The site was acquired by the public radio station WNYC in 1935 as the site of twin antennae used for broadcasting.  From 1937 to 1990, the city-operated station broadcast its AM signal from this location.  Following the adoption of antennae in Kearny, New Jersey and atop the World Trade Center, the Greenpoint property sat unused.  Ground was broken for WNYC Transmitter Park in August 2010, followed by four years of construction.

DZME | DZME (1530 kHz Metro Manila) is the flagship AM station owned by Capitol Broadcasting Center in the Philippines.  The station's studio is located at Unit 1802, 18/F, OMM-Citra Building, # 39 San Miguel Avenue, Ortigas Center, Pasig City, and its transmitter is located at # 78 Flamengco St., Brgy.  Panghulo, Obando, Bulacan.

WCKY (AM) | WCKY is an AM radio station in Cincinnati, Ohio, United States, broadcasting at 1530 kHz with 50,000 watts, and its transmitter is located in nearby Villa Hills, Kentucky.  It is a class A clear channel station, sharing the frequency with co-owned station KFBK in Sacramento, and can be heard, particularly at night, over most of the eastern U.S.  The station is owned by iHeartMedia and uses the on-air nickname "Cincinnati's ESPN 1530."

KFBK (AM) | KFBK (1530 AM) is a radio station in Sacramento, California broadcasting on a frequency of 1530 kHz.  KFBK is a class A station owned by iHeartMedia (formerly Clear Channel Communications).  It used to be designated as a class "I-B" station, sharing Class A status on 1530 AM with WCKY in Cincinnati, Ohio.  The station boasts the highest field strength of any AM station in the United States.  KFBK is also simulcast on KFBK-FM at 93.1 MHz.  The station has studios in North Sacramento near Arden Fair Mall, and its transmitter is near Pleasant Grove.

KBME (AM) | KBME (branded as Sportstalk 790) AM is a sports-talk radio station in the Houston, Texas metropolitan area.  It is currently owned by iHeartMedia, Inc..  The station airs local sports-talk and carries nationally syndicated Fox Sports Radio programming.  KBME is also the flagship radio station for the Houston Rockets, Houston Astros and Texas Longhorns.  The station's studios are located along the West Loop Freeway in the city's Uptown district.  The transmitter site is located at what is now the southwest corner of Fallbrook Drive and T.C. Jester Boulevard, near Greenspoint in unincorporated Harris County.

Orion, Illinois | Orion (The "Or" is pronounced "oar" and the "i" like a long "e") is a village in Henry County, Illinois, United States.  The population was 1,861 at the 2010 census.  It is the site for transmitters for many of the Quad Cities' radio and television stations, including KLJB, KWQC-TV, WMWC-TV, WQAD-TV, WQPT-TV and KQIN.  FM radio station transmitters include WLKU and WXLP.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - KQSP (passage [1]) — quotes: "licensed to Shakopee, Minnesota" and "transmitter site is in Chanhassen." (passage [1]).
  - Other 1530‑AM station passages present (not KQSP): DZME (passage [3]), WCKY (passage [4]), KFBK (passage [5]) — these show other stations/transmitter cities but are different stations (passages [3],[4],[5]).
- Ambiguities:
  - Multiple 1530 AM stations appear in the passages (DZME, WCKY, KFBK). Do not assume any of these — the question names KQSP specifically.
  - The expected city page for "Chanhassen" is not included in the provided passages (missing).
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity: Chanhassen (the city named in passage [1] as the transmitter site).
  - Missing fact needed: the population of Chanhassen, Minnesota at the 2010 U.S. census (exact census population number).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Chanhassen, Minnesota" (Wikipedia) — check the lede/infobox and the "Demographics" section for "2010 census" population. Search keywords: "Chanhassen population 2010 census".
  2) Re-open "KQSP" page (lede/infobox or "Transmitter" section) to confirm the transmitter-city wording if needed. Search keywords: "KQSP transmitter Chanhassen".
  3) If disambiguation needed, open "Chanhassen (disambiguation)" or the Minnesota city list to ensure correct municipal page.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Chanhassen, Minnesota | Chanhassen is a city in Carver and Hennepin counties in the state of Minnesota.  It is southwest of Minneapolis.  The population was 22,952 at the 2010 census.

Chanhassen High School | Chanhassen High School (CNS) is a public high school located in Chanhassen, Minnesota, a southwestern suburb of the Minneapolis-Saint Paul area.  CNS has a grade 9–12 school program.  Construction of the school was approved by voters in 2006 in response to rapidly growing enrollment in Carver County and overcrowding at Chaska High School, the district's other high school.  Chanhassen had an enrollment of 1,576 students during the 2014-15 school year, with an 18:1 student teacher ratio.  There are 82 teachers at the school, and more than 80 percent of them have a master's degree or higher.  The student body makeup is 52 percent male and 48 percent female, and the total minority enrollment is 9 percent.

Washington County, Minnesota | Washington County is a county located in the U.S. state of Minnesota.  As of the 2010 census, the population was 238,136, making it the fifth-most populous county in Minnesota.  Its county seat is Stillwater.  The largest city in the county is Woodbury, which had a population of 61,961 at the 2010 census.  The county was established in 1849.

Church of St. Hubertus | The Church of St. Hubertus is a historic church in Chanhassen, Minnesota, United States, built in 1887.  It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1982 for having local significance in the themes of "community planning and development" and "exploration/settlement".  It was nominated for symbolizing the Franciscan brotherhood that platted and grew the German Catholic settlement that became Chanhassen.

Rogers, Minnesota | Rogers is a city in Hennepin County, Minnesota, United States.  The population was 8,597 at the 2010 census.  However, the population increased to over 11,000 upon the annexing of surrounding Hassan Township in 2012.  The population of Hassan Township was 2,600 in 2010, giving the current city a population base of 11,197 as of the census.  The center of population of Minnesota is located in Rogers .  The city is a northwest suburb of the Minneapolis–Saint Paul metropolitan area.

Trimont, Minnesota | Trimont is a city in Martin County, Minnesota, USA.  The population was 747 at the 2010 census.  The U.S. Census Bureau estimated 2011 population is 740.  The small community is located in south central Minnesota between Sherburn and St. James on Minnesota State Highway 4.  Interstate 90 is nearby.
Welcome, Minnesota | Welcome is a city in Martin County, Minnesota, United States.  The population was 686 at the 2010 census.  The U.S. Census Bureau estimated 2012 population is 678.
response.summary_2: transmitter site is in Chanhassen. Chanhassen, Minnesota had a population of 22,952 at the 2010 census.
